data.title: Nike: Return To The Old Strategy
data.description:
Today Nike Inc. (NKE) is a classic story of a "fallen angel". Shares of one of the world leaders of sports equipment are trading near five-year lows, at the level of $52, having lost almost 21% of value just for the last year. The capitalization of the company shrunk to $77 billion. The market mercilessly discounts the shares, reacting to a series of weak quarterly reports and the loss of market share.

At the same time, behind the current pessimism of Wall Street hides a fundamental turnaround in the strategy of the company. In my view, the deep crisis of Nike is not a result of a sudden loss of the power of the brand or irreversible macroeconomic factors. This is a direct consequence of one, but monumental strategic mistake of management: an attempt to pull an IT-model of sales onto traditional fashion-retail. The realization of this mistake and the aggressive return to the classic model of distribution under the leadership of the new CEO creates a rare point of entry for long-term investors.

The profile of the company and the essence of the problem: The illusion of direct margin

Historically the empire of Nike was built on a total, aggressive presence everywhere — from premium boutiques to local multi-brand sports networks. The brand was everywhere. But there is a catch: the past leadership, headed by a tech sector veteran John Donahoe (ex-eBay, ServiceNow), decided to rewrite the rules of the game, having made a radical bet on the model Direct-to-Consumer (DTC) — direct sales to the consumer. On paper, in presentations for investors, the mathematics of this strategy seemed flawless and seductive. Why give away sneakers to a wholesaler for $50, losing control over the client experience, if one can sell them through one's own mobile application SNKRS or branded site directly to the final buyer for $100? In the chase for a multiple increase of gross margin Nike began to methodically and purposefully tear ten-year contracts with thousands of retailers (from independent stores to such giants, as Foot Locker) and aggressively drive traffic into its online-channel.

The error of the system: The specificity of the fashion industry and its consequences

Leadership ignored the fundamental rule of the industry of clothing and footwear, which is impossible to digitize: a brand lives, while it is in sight. Footwear is a complex, tactile product. The buyer wants to come into a store, hold the goods in his hands, try on the fit, compare with competitors. The consequence of blind faith in DTC and the exit from physical multi-brand retail became two fatal mistakes, which we right now see in the financial reports. The first problem became a vacuum on the shelves and the voluntary surrender of market share. Having left from physical retail, Nike literally with its own hands freed the best, the most prime retail real estate to its competitors. The buyer, coming into Foot Locker for the customary "swoosh" and not finding it, did not go to download the application of Nike. He bought that, which stood on the shelf: On Running, Hoka, New Balance. Having given away physical access to its loyal audience, Nike grew powerful competitors.

The second, no less destructive mistake is the death of innovations in favor to algorithms. The digital DTC-model made the company a hostage of its own data. The algorithms of sales showed the obvious: best of all, with the least expenses on marketing, classic retro-models (Jordan, Dunk, Air Force 1) are sold. Instead of investing into the development of new breakthrough technologies for professional athletes and runners, Nike began to endlessly "milk" old hits, re-releasing them in thousands of new color variations. The market quickly oversaturated itself with retro-silhouettes, exclusivity disappeared, and the status of the main "innovator" in sports was lost.

Financial retro-analysis: The price of managerial mistake

The consequences of this strategy reflected themselves in the financial reporting of the company. If we look at the trends of the last years, then one can see that this strategy led to bad results. The peak of success of the digital strategy fell on the 2023 and 2024 financial years. As is seen from the income statement, the total revenue of the company (Total Revenues) reached $51.2 billion and $51.3 billion respectively. Gross profit held on record levels of about $22.9 billion. Net profit in the 2024 year constituted an impressive $5.7 billion.

But already in the 2025 year the oversaturation of the market and the loss of wholesale channels led to a collapse of the indicators of the company. The total revenue of the company fell to $46.3 billion. In parallel with the fall of sales operational profit also fell to $3.2 billion in the 2025 year. However, the freshly released Q3 2026 earnings report shows that this freefall has finally found a bottom. While the company is still in the active stage of transformation, overall revenue stabilized at $11.3 billion (flat year-over-year). Profitability remains under temporary pressure as the company clears inventory and restructures, with EPS coming in at $0.35, but the bloodbath in sales volumes has clearly stopped.

Investment idea: Turnaround and return to the roots

Having realized the scale of the impending catastrophe, the board of directors of Nike pressed on the emergency brake. The return into leadership of the veteran of the company Elliot Hill in the capacity of CEO probably means a turnaround of strategy by 180 degrees. And now we observe a classic stage of recovery of the business, which bases itself on three fundamental directions. Firstly, the company actively restores torn ties with retailers and returns goods onto the physical shelves of multi-brand networks. Retail networks themselves are extremely interested in the return of Nike, since exactly this brand historically generated to them a good traffic. And we are already seeing the first green shoots of this strategy: according to the Q3 2026 report, wholesale revenues grew by 5% to $6.5 billion, while direct sales (NIKE Direct) continued their decline, dropping by 4%. This proves that the pivot is not just management rhetoric — it is already physically reflecting in the cash flows, with the wholesale segment stopping the overall decline and pulling sales upward. Secondly, a large-scale return to innovations is announced. There goes a shifting of focus from lifestyle and the stamping of "Jordans" back to sports, athletes and new running technologies. The news background, including the strategy "Win Now", confirms, that budgets on R&D again are being redirected to productivity. And thirdly, an aggressive cleaning of the balance is going on. The company methodically cleans warehouses from old reserves through discount channels, to free up liquidity and "give oxygen" to new lines. And I think, that as Nike sneakers again will massively appear in trading centers, the effect of scale will work, and the sagged revenue will begin to restore itself.

Modeling and quantitative calculation (Valuation)

The investment attractiveness bases itself on that fact, that the market values NKE by the inertia of its fall by current numbers. And really, at first glance, the forward multiplier P/E looks very high — 34.3 at a forecasted EPS around $1.54. But here is a nuance: this model lays down a linear continuation of the old strategy of the company with falling indicators. If though we look at the normalized ability of the business to generate profit, under a strategy in which the company will restore its lost positions then the picture cardinally changes. In the healthy 2024 year the company earned $5.7 billion of net profit. At the current capitalization of $77 billion this gives us a multiplier to normalized profit at the level of about 13.7. Historically (in the epoch before the DTC-crisis) Nike stably traded with a premium, having a P/E in the range of 25x–30x for the status of a global leader with a deep "economic moat". Assuming a conservative scenario, under which the new team will restore net profit to average values of $5.0 – $5.5 billion in the course of the next 18–24 months, and the market assigns to the company a fair, but restrained target P/E in the size of 20x, then in this case we can calculate on a target capitalization, equal to approximately 100 billion dollars. And this gives an excellent potential of growth equal to about 30% from the current levels. As an additional bonus one can consider a stable dividend yield in 3.10% ($1.64 per share).

Risks for the investment thesis

The transition from DTC back to the wholesale model of distribution inevitably means, that Nike will have to again share margin with middlemen. At the first stages this will put pressure on the gross profitability. The success of the investment idea entirely depends on the fact, whether management will be able to compensate the decrease of margin from one pair of footwear by a sharp growth of the total volumes of sales (the operating leverage). The main challenge remains the fact, that Nature abhors a vacuum. For the time of the voluntary absence of Nike on the key showcases, competitors did not sit idly by. The brands On, Hoka and the reborn New Balance managed not only to occupy the freed physical space in stores, but also to intercept the attention of buyers, having entrenched behind themselves the loyalty of a new audience. To return to the wholesaler — this is only the first step; now Nike will have to physically squeeze out entrenched competitors from the shelves. Retailers can resist the reduction of area for young brands, which right now bring them high margin and fresh traffic. The struggle for the showcase can demand from Nike unprecedented marketing budgets and an aggressive price policy, which will slow down the restoration of profit. Furthermore, one cannot ignore macroeconomic threats and internal delays. The development of truly new, innovative running lines requires time — usually the cycle of creation takes 18-24 months, and the market can lose patience, expecting breakthrough products. The global consumer also is under pressure: inflationary fatigue hits on discretionary expenses, and the situation on the key for Nike market of China remains complex due to weak internal demand. Finally, the process of aggressive clearing of reserves carries in itself a hidden reputational risk — too big a volume of goods in discount channels can wash out the premium perception of the brand.

Conclusion

Nike — this is a rare opportunity to buy an excellent sports brand with a discount due to a solvable managerial mistake. The return to the wholesale model — this is a fast method to restore the volumes of sales and return the lost market share. The internal metrics of the platform Seeking Alpha (among which Momentum historically is under pressure due to a drawn-out fall, and the factor Valuation finally began to shift into the green zone) confirm: the stock has shaken off the excessive hype. Current levels near $52 per share with a dividend yield above 3% provide investors a sufficient margin of safety. The strategy "Win Now" is launched, and the market for now underestimates the speed, with which Nike is capable to return to itself a place on the shelves.

data.title: LENZ Therapeutics: Speculative But Strong Buy
data.description:
Article Thesis

My first article about LENZ Therapeutics, Inc. (LENZ), dates from April of last year, when I initiated a small stock position (about 0.25x size) around $21, due to a high probability of approval for their aceclidine-based eye drop Vizz (ex LNZ100) for presbyopia. However, I moved to "Hold" in August, with the stock at $38-$39, after the FDA approval, and I trimmed my initially small position after nearly doubling. I did add back (through options this time) with the stock dropping at ~$18.

But why did I trim an already small position anyway? To mark close to 100% profit for part of my position for an extremely risky stock. These one-product stocks are almost always extremely risky; perhaps they are even less risky prior to approval, because the market is more optimistic while waiting for approval. But after that:

  • Many investors will just want to mark some profits (like me), considering the stock at least fairly valued (some might consider it even a bit overvalued).
  • Their product can experience some side effects after approval (more or less serious, more or less causal), with a much larger patient base.
  • Competition may arise, either from new pipeline drugs advancing or from some generic companies attacking their patent (causing painful legal actions).
  • Short sellers trying to exploit any of the above situations, as well as just trying to pressure down the price for an illiquid stock.

In this case, we experienced at least three out of the above four factors (the third one was already known). Therefore, I want to update my view and my position in the following.

Business Highlights

The most important event since my last article happened at the beginning of December, when the stock dropped ~26% in a single session due to a serious adverse effect (retinal tear) reported in the FDA's database. First, let's clarify a little bit the circumstances of this case:

  • That patient had a history of retinal problems, and he did not take a retinal examination before using Vizz. This is something already mentioned on their label: Vizz should be taken only after a retinal examination.
  • I heard opinions that this is a difficult extra step; however, I disagree. This is a relatively simple exam, and patients with presbyopia need to go anyway for an ophthalmology consult every two to three years. OK, this is not something urgent indeed, maybe a reason why not all presbyopes will suddenly hurry in the first year for their retinal exams.
  • The patient has been treated, so fortunately, this has not been an irreversible case.
  • I didn't hear of any drug or treatment with an immaculate zero on the serious side effects column; however, the probability in Vizz's case is very low.

However, I do think there is a combination of misinformation and, perhaps, a bit of justified fear for a treatment with even distant chances of more serious side effects like this one that will keep some of its possible buyers away.

During their last earnings call, LENZ's CEO noted that "the number of prescribing doctors we have seen at this stage of the launch already significantly exceeds what we observed with several recent eye care product launches." On the other hand, management identified the need to change the mindset of physicians who primarily think of the product just for early presbyopes towards a broader category.

From my point of view, refill rates are the most important thing that we need to watch for this year. Like I said, this is not an urgent treatment; therefore, I don't expect all presbyopes to hurry up for their retinal exam. So many of them could start in 2027 and beyond, but for the refill rates, I think we could already have a good picture this year, with management telling us that "actual refill percentages would be communicated in the second half of the year."

Fair Value

Base-Case Scenario

In my previous articles, I started from the company's "conservative" market TAM estimate of $3B, and then I assigned it a market share, considering their competition. However, I think I have today more elements to do my own detailed estimates, and I don't know anyway if that initial "conservative" estimate is still conservative after that serious side effect reported.

For the United States, my 10-year estimates are based on the following steps:

  1. With about 83M Americans aged 45-64, and about 86% living in metropolitan areas, we have about 71.4M people in Vizz's main target group.
  2. There are estimates for presbyopia between 83.0% and 88.9% for adults aged 45 and older; let's take only 83% for the 45-64 group, which gives us ~$59.2M people.
  3. There are about 25% of Americans aged 35-44 earning $100K/year or more, and a bit more (25%-30%) for the 45-64 group. By taking 28%, we filtered down our target group to 17M people.
  4. I will add a relatively modest 3M (17%-18%) from other groups: younger/older people, rural area residents, and people earning less than $100K/year (but close), for a total of 20M people.
  5. According to some estimates, about 71% of presbyopia patients actively seek alternatives to traditional corrective eyewear for various reasons: look (I saw some comments that this is almost the only reason, but it's not); convenience (I don't want to carry my glasses with me everywhere, at supermarkets, events, courses, etc.); physical activities like swimming, sports, and job-related activities; and avoidance of multi-focal lenses. Let's be a bit conservative and take only 60%, which gives us 12M people.
  6. We need to filter just the patients without problems at a retinal examination. From what I found on Google, 90%+ are suitable for Vizz with a healthy retina. By taking 90%, we have 10.8M people.
  7. I now add another filter (perhaps the company also needs to add it for their own estimates) for people who would like Vizz but are afraid of side effects (it does not matter if they are real or misinformation). I must say that this is, probably, the most difficult estimate to make; it could be 20% or 80%. I will just take an average of 50% for 5.4M people.
  8. Like mentioned in my first article, Vizz is far superior to AbbVie's Vuity, launched in December 2021, because Vuity can induce myopia. Besides, Vizz is potentially better tolerated by only minimally affecting the ciliary muscle, and it has a longer window of clear vision. A more powerful competitor will probably be Opus Genetics' new treatment. Leaving some room for other possible new treatments until 2035, I keep the same 30% market share estimate for 1.62M people.
  9. Let's further suppose that after any mild side effect, some people will just discontinue Vizz. From clinical trials, we have side effects reported for about 10%-20% of participants. Let's suppose that only ~80% will continue with Vizz longer-term, which will give us 1.3M customers, which is a bit more than 0.3% of the US population.
  10. Let's now split them into two groups: more constant customers (mostly the ones interested in looks or trying to avoid bifocal lenses) and occasional customers (we can think of people doing swimming/sports 2-3 times/week or the ones seeking Vizz for convenience in different circumstances), for an average of about 6 months/year (we'll have a better idea later this year after they report actual refill rates).
  11. The company has long-standing expectations of ~$60/month net price, but I model an average of $55/month, considering the lower-priced three-month packs. That gives us 1.3M people X 6 months X $55/month = $429M. By adding the one-time customers, I have about $450M estimated for 2035.

With about $1.6M net revenues in their first quarter, I model a relatively modest $12M for the whole year 2026, because they rely heavily on sampling in the beginning. From $12M in 2026 to $450M in 2035, I model a normal trend with higher growth in the first half, with an aggressive marketing campaign, followed by slower growth in the second half for a more mature product.

Lenz has also advanced with their partnerships in other regions:

  • Partnership with Laboratoires Théa (Canada) for an upfront payment of $2.5M (received), up to $67.5M in milestone payments (I model the first one in 2026 considering a submission in Canada, and I model a total of $60M to be a bit conservative), and tiered double-digit royalties (I will just model 15%, because the population base is very low). To be a bit conservative, I will take only 0.2% from the total population, for 80K customers, and $50 per month, for $36M peak sales.
  • Partnership with Corxel Pharmaceuticals (Greater China) for up to $95M in development and milestone payments (they already received $10M in 2025, so I model the next one only in 2027, and in total I model $70M to be a bit conservative) and tiered mid-single-digit to low double-digit royalties (I will model from 15% to 6%). I model an even lower 0.1% population due to cultural/religious/political differences for 3M customers and only $20 per month, with purchasing power in China about 3 times lower than in the US but increasing.
  • Partnership with Lotus Pharmaceutical (South Korea, Thailand, Philippines, Vietnam, Malaysia, Brunei, Indonesia, and Singapore) for a $5M upfront payment (received), up to $120M in regulatory and milestone payments (I model the next one in 2026, considering some submissions/approvals, and a total of $100M to be a bit conservative), and tiered double-digit royalties (I model from 15% to 10%, although they can be up to 20%). I model the same 0.1% population like for China, due to cultural/religious differences, for 685K customers, and only $15/month, with a much lower purchasing power than in the US (except for South Korea and Singapore).
  • Partnership with Lunatus (Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain, Oman, Jordan, Lebanon, and Iraq). While I couldn't find the exact terms of their agreement, I model a total of $60M in regulatory and milestone payments, proportional with the other agreements. With 137K potential customers considering 0.2% penetration. I model $40/month (considering some richer countries) and 15% royalties.

The most important region outside the US is, obviously, the EU. While they don't have a partnership for now, Lenz submitted the marketing authorization application to the EMA. However, let's not model anything for this region. Besides the fact that we don't know yet the terms of a future partnership, for all these other regions outside the US, we should model a probability factor, since Vizz is still to be approved. Therefore, I consider 100% probability of adoption in the above 4 regions and 0% in the EU, which I think gives a conservative average, considering the relatively large population and the high purchasing power in the EU.

And of course, they should be able to subsequently apply for adoption in other interesting geographies, either rich countries (Japan, Australia, New Zealand) or populous countries (India, Brazil, Mexico), so I think I am on the conservative side regarding their international revenues.

Switching to profitability, I model a 77% terminal gross margin for their US operations, in line (a bit conservative) with other small biotech companies. Then, they had $39.6M in SG&A expenses in Q4'25, and I model a slight increase to $170-$180M for 2026-2027, each with four full quarters, then a slight decline to $170M in 2028 (they should already have all their international partnerships), declining again to $155M in 2029 (they should have finished their DTC campaign), then only minor declines considering a more mature product. Finally, I model them gradually restarting modest R&D from 2029, when they should be cash-flow positive.

Their tax rate could vary, of course, but I model an average of 24%, close to a maximum, because they should have low R&D credits:

My other assumptions for a DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) model are:

  • -3% growth in perpetuity. This gives us revenues halved in about twenty years starting from 2035, which I find normal, considering that their patent will expire somewhere in the middle of this interval.
  • 12.5% discount rate (I generally use 12%-13% for these small biotech companies with only one product due to their very high risk).

Resulting Fair Value is $25, about 198% potential upside from the current price ($8.4).

Reverse-DCF (Pessimistic) Scenario

For a reverse-DCF model, let's try to model what inputs would be necessary for a Fair Value around the current price:

  • For Step 7 in my filtering process (which I mentioned is the most difficult to estimate), let's suppose that only 20% of people are not afraid of taking Vizz (instead of 50%), even after successfully passing a retinal examination.
  • For Step 8, I consider only 25% market share for Vizz, which gives us ~432K customers, or about $150M US peak sales in 2035.
  • I model all their international partnerships starting much later, in 2028 (and with lower total payments).
  • After two years with those very elevated SG&A but struggling sales growth, willingly or unwillingly, they should give up their DTC sooner, and after 2029 they should readjust SG&A as a percentage of their sales anyway:

I model another change, -5% growth in perpetuity, considering that they will have generic competition starting in 2035, even before their patent expiration (these things sometimes happen, although it's not usual).

With these assumptions, Fair Value estimate is $8.45, but I, personally, see at least that 20% filter from Step 7 as too conservative.

Short-Term Signals

There were some insiders buying in November in the low twenties, and I would normally count that as a bullish signal, since it happened in the last ~6 months. However, in this case, that was exactly before that negative event with the retinal tear. I would like to see insiders buying at the very depressed prices today; therefore, I consider this signal as neutral.

If I look at insider activity, I also look at the short interest ratio, and we see it increasing almost immediately after the FDA approval (like mentioned in the introduction, probably based on valuation) and then increasing again after that event in December. Considering a low market cap stock (about $260M), shorts have some power to manipulate the price regardless of any fundamentals; therefore, I consider this as a minor negative signal.

We can also have interesting hints from the options market. For example, a Put-Call Open Interest Ratio of 0.54 is normally considered bullish because there are more Call options than Put options, and the trend is down. However, with just two relevant expiration dates (Jul'26 and Jan'27), I consider this only as a minor positive signal:

In conclusion, with a minor negative signal and a minor positive one, I rely strictly on the 10-year DCF-based Fair Value estimate.

Strategy

Like mentioned in the introduction, I had a significant realized gain for part of my initial stock position immediately after the FDA approval, but this was offset by the loss from the bullish options strategy initiated with the stock around $18 (just before that negative event from December…that's why I like to keep the position size small for these one-product companies).

I want to focus now on my last (and current) neutral-to-bullish position, described through this comment. I sold some 15 Put options expiring Jul'26 for an average of $2.39/share because IV (Implied Volatility) was high and the premium seemed intriguing. With the stock today at $8.4, I obviously have a significant loss of ~$4.21/share.

However, with my (base-case) Fair Value estimate around $25 and considering the small exposure, I will just hold longer-term, hoping for a recovery.

  • For now, I am still holding my short Put options because they still give me a bit of extrinsic value and because a return to the strike price ($15) would give me a substantial profit (the initially collected premium).
  • With the Put options close to the expiration and very close to zero extrinsic value, if the stock is still around these levels or lower, I will switch to a more bullish strategy, perhaps buying shares, targeting just a small exposure (but I can let the position grow to around a double before trimming).
  • With close to zero extrinsic value but the stock back towards $15 (maybe above $12 or so), I would rather sell again some Put options (probably the same $15 strike price) for some fat premium from a neutral-to-bullish strategy.
  • With the stock clearly above $15 after realizing that fat profit from the Put options, it would approach the fairly valued area, and, considering the extreme uncertainty, I will pass.

Risks

These small, one-product companies always come with extreme uncertainty. I mean, the stock could be at $40, or it could be at $8; there is always the risk for it to go to zero. And I think the main risk here is the possibility of severe side effects. And not like that case with the retinal tear, I am talking now after taking all the steps on the label, like a retinal exam, and an irreversible severe problem.

However, I also saw some bearish opinions that there were many other side effects reported in the FDA database, and I'd like to address them here:

  • Most of them are "Vitreous Detachment." I am not an expert, and if there is any expert reading this, please correct me if I am wrong. A vitreous detachment is not only much less serious than a retinal tear but is also relatively normal for presbyopes, especially after a certain age, so there is a high probability for a coincidence.
  • Besides that already known retinal tear problem, there were three other cases with "Vision Impairment." This is a serious problem, but we still can't see if the subjects performed that required retinal exam and/or if they had past problems, otherwise…
  • Another bearish argument is that the company didn't present these new side effects. First, we need to understand that this FDA database is open for anybody to post anything. Even more, there is no demonstrated causal relationship between the product and the symptom, like in the clinical trials.
  • But let's suppose that those 3-4 cases were related to Vizz; that's a possibility. There were 20000 prescriptions only in Q4'25, and probably 30000-40000 until the end of February. I would say that 3-4 out of 30000-40000, meaning 0.01%, is a very low rate of occurrence, and this is on the label. Like I said, I didn't hear of any drug with an immaculate zero for more serious side effects.

But true problem or coincidence, it doesn't matter too much for an optional treatment; that's why I modeled in my Step 7 the fear factor.

Another possible risk affecting these one-product companies is an attack from a generic manufacturer before their patent expiration. That can happen and, at the very least, can trigger some years of legal battles and, for sure, another short-term attack from the shorts, regardless of the long-term resolution. But this has low probability; that's why I modeled it only in my pessimistic scenario.

With ~$292M in cash & equivalents, they will probably need to raise more cash only once and only by late 2027–early 2028, according to my model (or earlier if they can get a high price). But this is a risk with unknown magnitude; there is a big difference between issuing new shares at $40 and issuing them at $6 (of course, there are other possible means of financing besides issuing new shares).

In conclusion, only a speculative buy, because there is always extreme uncertainty with these one-product companies. That's why I initiate only a small position from a total of about 10 small biotech names, and these ~10 make only about 10% of my bullish portfolio.

But a strong buy, because the reward/risk ratio is very favorable. With my base-case Fair Value of $25, we have ~200% potential gain (compared to a maximum of 100% potential loss, and that with lower probability anyway). If we add some other international revenues (we'll also have a better idea this year), the potential reward would probably be 250%.

By "averaging" the "speculative" buy with the "strong" one, I rate it just a Buy.

Takeaway

Like any one-product company, LENZ Therapeutics is a very high-risk name, with a major risk from possible serious side effects post-label compliance and minor-to-moderate risks, including generic competition before patent expiry and the need for capital raises at depressed share prices.

However, the potential reward is even higher. I estimate a bit conservative base-case Fair Value of $25 per share, implying ~200% upside from current levels (~$8.4), driven by US and existing international partnerships. With likely more international partnerships in the future, potential upside could be ~250%.

Management will report actual refill percentages in the second half of the year, which will clarify part of the long-term demand. Another big uncertainty is, however, the fear of even mild side effects for an optional product.

Investment Approach

  • Fidelity Freedom ® Funds (the Funds) are designed so that the target date referenced in the Fund name is the approximate year when investors expect to retire.
  • Except for Fidelity Freedom ® Retirement Fund, each of the Funds seeks high total return until reaching its respective target retirement date; thereafter, each Fund's objective will be to seek high current income and, as a secondary objective, capital appreciation.
  • Except for Fidelity Freedom ® Retirement Fund, each Fund's asset allocation strategy becomes increasingly diversified as it approaches its target date – and beyond. Ultimately, the Funds are expected to merge with Fidelity Freedom Retirement Fund.
  • The Funds employ a disciplined and time-tested investment process focused on helping investors achieve successful retirement outcomes by leveraging the depth and strength of Fidelity's investment research and resources.

Fund Information

Manager(s): Team Managed

Trading Symbol: FFFHX

Start Date: June 01, 2006

Size (in millions): $22,375.45

Morningstar Category: Fund Target-Date 2050

Investment performance of the Fidelity Freedom Fund products depends on the performance of the underlying investment options and on the proportion of the assets invested in each underlying investment option. The investment risk of each Fidelity Freedom Fund changes over time as its asset allocation changes. These risks are subject to the asset allocation decisions of the Investment Adviser. Pursuant to the Adviser's ability to use an active asset allocation strategy, investors may be subject to a different risk profile compared to the fund's neutral asset allocation strategy shown in its glide path. The funds are subject to the volatility of the financial markets, including that of equity and fixed income investments in the U.S. and abroad, and may be subject to risks associated with investing in high-yield, small-cap, commodity-linked and foreign securities. Leverage can increase market exposure, magnify investment risks, and cause losses to be realized more quickly. No target date fund is considered a complete retirement program and there is no guarantee any single fund will provide sufficient retirement income at or through retirement. Principal invested is not guaranteed at any time, including at or after the funds' target dates.

Total returns are historical and include change in share value and reinvestment of dividends and capital gains, if any. Cumulative total returns are reported as of the period indicated.

For definitions and other important information, please see the Definitions and Important Information section of this Fund Review.

Market Update and Portfolio Review

  • Global equities advanced in Q4 amid economic expansion and strong corporate fundamentals. Non-U.S. equities led the broad-based rally amid improved manufacturing conditions and policies to support domestic growth and consumption. The U.S. Federal Reserve cut interest rates twice in Q4, given signs of softer employment conditions. Inflation remained above target. Nominal 10-year U.S. Treasury bond yields finished Q4 effectively unchanged at around 4.2%. Credit spreads remained low relative to history.

  • Each Fidelity target-date strategy produced a gain in Q4. Performance as of year-end is displayed below (net of fees)*.

Performance Attribution Summary

  • Active asset allocation positioning and security selection added value to the fund's performance versus the Composite index in Q4. The fund's overweight in non-U.S. equities meaningfully contributed, as investments in both developed-markets equities and emerging-markets equities outperformed U.S. equities during the quarter.
  • The impact of active security selection varied by asset class. The strong performance of U.S. large-cap managers resulted in a relative contribution from U.S. equities, whereas non-U.S. managers, in aggregate, underperformed the Composite index.

Relative Contributors (representative across vintages):

  • Large-cap U.S. equity funds stood out to the upside this quarter, including the following notable relative contributors:
    • Fidelity® Series Growth Company Fund outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 3000® Growth Index. The fund is managed by Steve Wymer, who invests across a spectrum of companies, from blue-chip growth to aggressive growth. Steve focuses on firms operating in well-positioned industries and niches, and those he considers capable of delivering persistent sales and earnings growth. Security selection within the information technology sector contributed to the fund's relative result this quarter, especially out-of-benchmark positions in SanDisk (SNDK) and Ciena (CIEN), from the technology hardware & equipment industry. A non-benchmark position in private company Space Exploration Technologies, a maker of spacecraft and provider of satellite communications and launch capabilities, also notably contributed.
    • Fidelity® Series Large Cap Stock Fund outperformed its benchmark, the S&P 500® index. The fund, managed by Matt Fruhan, emphasizes companies for which he believes long-term normalized earnings and free cash flow are underappreciated by investors. Security selection contributed to the fund's relative result, notably within the industrials and financials sectors. Within financials, overweight positions in Wells Fargo and Bank of America added relative value, while larger-than-benchmark stakes in GE Vernova (GEV) and United Parcel Service (UPS) stood out in the industrials sector.

Relative Detractor (representative across vintages):

  • The largest fund-level detractor:
    • Fidelity® Series Overseas Fund underperformed its benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Index. The fund is managed by Vincent Montemaggiore, who is valuation sensitive and focuses on higher-quality companies with durable growth. Security selection detracted this quarter, particularly within the industrials sector. Within the sector, an overweight in Thales, a French company that provides advanced technologies, services and products to the defense, aerospace, space and cyber markets, hurt the fund's relative result most. An out-of-benchmark position in Baycurrent, one of Japan's largest consulting firms, also notably detracted, as did an overweight stake in Japanese food and biotechnology company Ajinomoto.

Active Asset Allocation:

  • The fund outperformed the Composite index for the quarter, driven by active asset allocation positioning.

Outlook and Positioning

  • Target-date funds are long-term strategies designed to help keep participants on track toward their retirement goals through different market environments. Strategic asset allocation decisions are focused on providing age-appropriate asset allocation throughout participants' changing time horizons.
  • Our research highlights several macro-oriented themes that we believe underscore the need for portfolio diversification. Government debt, global fragmentation, aging demographics and new technology are likely to impact long-term growth and inflation. We believe diversification in fixed income and inflation-resistant assets remains attractive to hedge these risks.
  • In 2025, we announced an update to the glide path and strategic asset allocation of the strategies, based on our long-term views of participant behavior and our capital markets research. We increased exposure to equities for early-career investors and to equities and inflation-sensitive assets for investors near and in retirement. Implementation of the updated allocations is underway, and we expect to complete the transition within the next six to 12 months.
  • Active asset allocation positioning relative to the strategic benchmark reflects our intermediate-term views on assets that are mispriced relative to our estimate of fair value. Active risk in the funds is at the low end of the historical range, based on a view that elevated asset valuations, particularly for U.S. stocks, provide little cushion against near-term economic and geopolitical risk. We believe valuations outside of the AI epicenter are reasonable, and many sectors have improving growth expectations.
  • As of the end of 2025, the funds were overweight equities, albeit by a smaller magnitude since the start of the quarter. We maintain a preference for non-U.S. assets relative to U.S. assets, based on attractive valuations and the potential for non-U.S. assets to benefit from continued U.S. dollar weakness.
  • We added to fixed-income investments in the fourth quarter, and we view intermediate-term U.S. Treasuries as the most attractive part of the Treasury yield curve. The Funds remain underweight investment-grade bonds, as spreads are low relative to history.
  • Inflation remains sticky and upside risk may be underappreciated by investors. Supply-side indicators, including housing and labor markets, pressure the intermediate-term horizon, in our view. As a result, in Q4 we added to the funds' overweight in commodities.

Dow Jones U.S. Total Stock Market Index is a float-adjusted market-capitalization-weighted index of all equity securities of U.S. headquartered companies with readily available price data.

MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (Net MA Tax) is a market-capitalization-weighted index designed to measure the investable equity market performance for global investors of large and mid-cap stocks in developed and emerging markets, excluding the United States. Index returns are adjusted for tax withholding rates applicable to U.S. based mutual funds organized as Massachusetts business trusts.

Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) 0-5 Year Index (Series-L) is a market value-weighted index that measures the performance of inflation-protected securities issued by the US Treasury that have a remaining average life of less than 5 years.

Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) 5+ Year Index (Series-L) is a market value-weighted index that measures the performance of inflation-protected securities issued by the US Treasury that have a remaining average life of 5 or more.

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based, market-value-weighted benchmark that measures the performance of the investment grade, U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market. Sectors in the index include Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, MBS (agency fixed-rate and hybrid ARM pass-throughs), ABS, and CMBS.

Bloomberg U.S. Long Treasury is a market value-weighted index of investment-grade fixed-rate public obligations of the U.S. Treasury with maturities of ten years or more.

Bloomberg Global Aggregate Treasury Ex USD Ex EM RIC Capped Float Adjusted Index (Hedged USD) is a customized subset of the Global Aggregate Treasury Index that meets the same diversification guidelines that a fund must pass to qualify as a regulated investment company (RIC). This multi-currency

The index also adjusts the par amount outstanding of GBP-, JPY-, and CAD-denominated bonds for Central Bank purchases in each of the respective currencies. The index returns are hedged to the U.S. dollar.

Bloomberg U.S. 3-6 Month Treasury Bills Index is a market-capitalization-weighted index of investment-grade, fixed-rate public obligations of the U.S. Treasury with remaining maturities from 3 up to (but not including) 6 months, excluding zero coupon strips.

Bloomberg Commodity 3 Month Forward Index is a version of the Bloomberg Commodity (BCOM) Index where the lead and future contra looks 3 months ahead of the BCOM index contract calendar. The index rebalances annually and is calculated in total return.

IMPORTANT FUND INFORMATION

Relative positioning data presented in this commentary is based on the fund's primary benchmark (Index) unless a secondary benchmark is provided to assess performance.

There are multiple drivers of performance for the Funds. Strategic asset allocation is expected to be the primary source of returns over long-term periods, while incremental excess return can result from active asset allocation and building-block manager decisions. Strategic allocation decisions are based on research and insights related to the factors that influence outcomes over long-term periods, including diversification, capital markets and participant behaviors. We periodically increase the diversification of our strategic asset allocation in an attempt to better navigate capital market uncertainty. Active asset allocation is intended to provide shareholders with exposure to asset classes that are mispriced and may offer additional returns and opportunities for risk mitigation. Active allocation expands the investment opportunity set and provides flexibility to adjust asset class exposures over intermediate-term periods. Allocations to active building-block managers offer shareholders the opportunity to benefit from informational advantages across Fidelity's asset management platform.

3-YEAR RISK/RETURN STATISTICS

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (less) than 1 is more (less) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. R-Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.
